retrieved_docs.title: Cherry Red Spot -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: GM2 gangliosidosis type 2 or Sandhoff disease is due to mutation of the beta subunit of hexosaminidase at chromosome 5q13. Reports describe infantile, juvenile, and adult forms. It is inherited autosomal recessively. The cherry-red spot is present in a majority of cases. Clinical features are similar to Tay-Sachs disease. Commotio retinae - After blunt trauma, acutely retinal opacification may occur. [10] When this retina whitening involves the macula, it is called Berlin's edema. Although actual retinal thickening is not present, this macular whitening can cause a pseudo-cherry red spot.

retrieved_docs.title: Cherry Red Spot -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: In lipid storage disorders, there might be evidence of neurodegeneration and visceromegaly. In the early phase, there is a perifoveal white donut-like area around the fovea with a cherry-red spot appearance. In the late phase, there is atrophy of inner retinal layers, the disappearance of the cherry-red spot, and optic atrophy. Family history and examination of family members are of paramount importance. Commotio retinae patients have a history of blunt trauma. Signs of blunt trauma, including hyphema, anterior uveitis, and damage to the seven rings of trauma, may be visible. Retinitis may be associated with intraocular inflammation, vitritis, and retinal necrosis that is more prominently visible on the optical coherence tomography scan. [26] [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Cherry Red Spot -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The possible differential diagnoses of cherry red spot include Macular hole Macular pseudohole Laser injury to the fovea Macular telangiectasia type 2 Choroidal neovascular membrane Macular hemorrhage [40]

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Child Abuse, Maltreatment, and Neglect -- History and Physical -- Bruising
retrieved_docs.content: A pediatrician needs to differentiate bruises from "pseudo-bruises," including Mongolian spots (grey-blue, can be found anywhere on the body with clear-cut margins), maculae cerulae (bluish spots on the skin due to pubic lice), and allergic shiners (more brown than blue, may be confused with black eyes). Bleeding disorders, vasculitides, hypersensitivity syndromes, and various cultural practices such as spooning, coining, and cupping are also included in the differential diagnoses for bruises.

retrieved_docs.title: Cherry Red Spot -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of a cherry-red spot at the macula varies according to the cause. Most cases of commotion retinae resolve with time, and visual recovery is usually good. CRAO and macular infarction, on the other hand, has a poor visual prognosis, and these eyes may become painful blind eyes due to neovascular glaucoma. Cases with progressive outer retinal necrosis (PORN) usually lose perception of light. Tay Sachs disease is usually fatal by five years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Cherry Red Spot -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Retinitis patients will need individualized therapy based on the clinical characteristics, imaging features, and results of investigations. [26] [27] [28] [37] [38] Patients with commotio retinae or Berlin's edema should be examined thoroughly to look for traumatic optic neuropathy, uveitis, hyphema, iris-sphincter tears, iridodialysis, retinal dialysis, and other evidence of trauma. The associated ocular injuries should be treated and may need topical or systemic steroids. Most cases with commotio retinae resolve spontaneously. [39] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Cherry Red Spot -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: As the color of choroid and RPE varies with racial variation, the color of the foveola or the central dot may change according to race. A true cherry-red spot presents in whites. The color of foveola was brown, causing a cherry brown spot in a Canadian aboriginal child with Sandhoff disease, and black in a patient of east Indian race creating a cherry black spot in Sandhoff disease. [21] Thus an alternate name of 'perifoveal white patch' has been suggested. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Cherry Red Spot -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of a cherry red spot at the macula will depend on the cause. In CRAO, atrophy of the retina and optic disc usually ensues in the long term. Commotio retinae may rarely be followed by pigmentary retinopathy and retinal thinning. In cases with lipid storage disorder, optic nerve pallor and retinal thinning may appear as time progresses. Retinal detachments may complicate cases with necrotizing retinitis.

retrieved_docs.content: "TEN 4" is a useful mnemonic to recall which bruising locations are of concern in cases involving physical abuse: bruises of the torso, ear, or neck in a child younger than 4 years of age or any bruising in a child younger than 4 months of age concern for abuse. Other injuries that are highly suggestive of abuse include: Retinal hemorrhages Posterior rib fractures Classic metaphyseal lesions [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Cherry Red Spot -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Other causes of retinal whitening include retinitis and trauma (commotion retinae). Sphingolipidoses are disorders of lysosomal metabolism, which involve conjugated products of ceramide and sugar or phospholipids. Sphingolipidoses include gangliosidosis, Niemann-Pick disease, Farber disease, and metachromatic leukodystrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: Cherry Red Spot -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: A cherry-red spot refers to a red-tinted region at the center of the macula surrounded by retinal opacification. Cherry-red spots at the macula may be present in various pathologic conditions, including lysosomal storage disorders, retinal ischemia, and retinal infarction. Ruling out life-threatening or sight-threatening diseases is of paramount importance in a patient with a cherry-red spot at the macula. Ruling out serious life-threatening or sight-threatening diseases is crucial in a patient with a cherry-red spot at the macula. The management varies according to the cause. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of patients with cherry-red spots. It highlights the role of interprofessional team members in collaborating to provide well-coordinated care and enhance patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 611
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical substrate for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies, including acute vascular events such as choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي) and choroidal tear (التمزق المشيمي). It is the primary site of numerous inflammatory disorders: disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, peripheral disseminated choroiditis, chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis, retinochoroiditis, unspecified disseminated retinochoroiditis (التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد), choroiditis (التهاب المشيمية), combined choroiditis and retinitis (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية), and acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE, اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد). These inflammatory processes may involve the posterior pole or be widespread. Post‑injury and post‑procedure sequelae such as postinflammatory chorioretinal scar, postsurgical choroid scar, posttraumatic choroid scar, and retinal choroidal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) demonstrate healing‑related lesions within the choroid. Degenerative and calcific changes include secondary choroidal atrophy spread (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين), sclerotic degeneration (تنكس حصوي), generalized choroidal calcification around the globe (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in hemorrhagic, traumatic, inflammatory, postoperative, and degenerative eye diseases, guiding clinicians in diagnosis, monitoring, and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) serves as the anatomic locus for a wide spectrum of ocular disorders, encompassing inflammatory, traumatic, degenerative, metastatic, and congenital conditions. Disseminated choroiditis, as well as the combined entity disseminated choroiditis AND chorioretinitis involving the posterior pole, are inflammatory processes that manifest within the choroid. Similar inflammatory involvement is seen in focal choroiditis and in metastatic disseminated retinitis AND retinochoroiditis, indicating that both primary ocular inflammation and secondary metastatic spread can target the choroidal tissue. Scarring of the choroid and retina is also common; disseminated scar of retina AND choroid, post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar, and post‑traumatic choroid scar all represent reparative or fibrotic sequelae that localize to the choroidal layer after injury or inflammation. Degenerative changes are represented by partial generalized choroidal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) serves as the anatomic site for a wide spectrum of ocular pathologies, encompassing inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, metastatic, degenerative, and congenital disorders. Inflammatory involvement includes disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, and disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis of the posterior pole. Scarring processes are represented by disseminated scar of retina and choroid and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar. Retinal‑choroidal inflammatory overlap is seen in metastatic disseminated retinitis and retinochoroiditis. Several conditions described in Arabic also localize to the choroidea. Acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد (APMPPE)) is an inflammatory placoid disorder of the choroid and retinal pigment epithelium.
